data.title: Capstone Copper: Some Near-Term Turbulence, But Good Long-Term Value
data.description:
Overview

Capstone Copper (CS:CA) is a copper mining company listed in both Canada and Australia. It produces almost exclusively copper, unlike many other mining companies that often mine more of a mix of metals.

The company has 4 producing mines in Chile, Mexico, and the U.S. There is also the Santo Domingo development project, where the company recently divested 25% to Orion Resource Partners LP, not to overextend its balance sheet, taking this project to production.

Production

Capstone did, during the last week, release its Q4 2025 and full-year production numbers. Q4 was another record quarter, and the company came in at the lower end of the annual production guidance range.

Cozamin has performed in line with guidance during 2025, while Mantoverde and Pinto Valley have underperformed slightly. Mantoverde experienced some unplanned downtime in the September-November period due to a ball mill motor failure and maintenance following that. With that said, we can see in the chart below that the throughput in December was above design levels, which is an encouraging sign for the future.

Mantos Blancos had a good year, with production above the annual guidance range due to strong throughput in a couple of quarters and higher grades.

Growth

The company has yet to provide official guidance for the year, but production is likely to be softer in the beginning of 2026 by the looks of things. Capstone is currently in a labor dispute with one of the unions at Mantoverde, which has gone on strike.

Capstone did last year "successfully negotiate new three-year collective bargaining agreements with the three other unions at Mantoverde," and the higher copper price has likely provided this union with more leverage in the negotiations. Production at Mantoverde is expected to be running at 50-75% capacity during this strike.

This will have a material impact on the consolidated financials in the near term, given that Mantoverde has accounted for roughly 50% of adjusted EBITDA lately.

The near-term growth for Capstone is from the Mantoverde Optimized project ("MV-O"), which can take production up towards 280 kt per year. MV-O has a very manageable capital cost of $176M. This can be financed from the existing liquidity position and ongoing cash flow, especially if the strong copper price holds going forward. The project is currently under construction, with ramp-up scheduled for late 2026 or early 2027.

The main source of growth for Capstone is otherwise the Santo Domingo project, which can take production up towards 400 kt per year, and it is expected to be sanctioned in the second half of 2026. This is a more sizable project, though, with an initial capital cost estimated to be $2.3B.

There are plenty of other sources of growth that the company is looking at with the existing producing assets, even if I suspect Capstone will be busy with MV-O and Santo Domingo over the next couple of years. I do think the list below highlights the potential in Capstone.

Valuation & Conclusion

Using the latest share price of Capstone and the financials as of Q3 2025, the company has a market cap of $8.0B and an enterprise value of $9.0B. The estimated EBITDA is between $1.5B and $2.0B over the next few years for Capstone, which gives us a forward-looking EV to EBITDA of 4.5-6.0.

This is, in my view, an attractive value for this quality producer with all its production in the Americas. The estimates have increased over the last 6 months as the copper price has rallied, but I highly doubt the latest estimates reflect the latest copper price. So, the EBITDA estimates will likely continue to increase if copper prices remain firm.

The stock price performance of Capstone has done well over the last year, up 72%, due to the strong copper price. However, we can see from the chart below that Capstone has underperformed the Global X Copper ETF (COPX) over this period.

The downtime at Mantoverde during September-November and now a strike at its flagship mine are likely the main reasons for the recent underperformance. I would suggest zooming out here and focusing on the solid historical operating performance, the good jurisdictions, and growth projects. So, I rate Capstone as a buy, and even if the stock hasn't corrected much, I view the relative underperformance as a decent buying opportunity.

All mining companies carry risks as well. There are jurisdictional risks, even if Capstone has better jurisdictions than many other copper mining companies in my view. There are execution risks taking both MV-O and Santo Domingo to production, even if MV-O is a much smaller project where I am not overly concerned.

Capstone had $623M in attributable net debt at the end of Q3 2025, and the financial leverage will increase once Santo Domingo is commissioned, but the choice to divest 25% for up to $360M means the balance sheet will likely not get overextended. The very strong copper prices we have seen lately will no doubt boost earnings and cash flow, but they will also increase the liquidity position materially, mitigating any potential balance sheet risks the market might have for this growth company.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ardelyx: IBSRELA Momentum Drives Opportunity
data.description:
Ardelyx (ARDX) has developed into a commercial execution machine with two FDA-approved, first-in-class medications. Coming into 2026, Ardelyx has a dual-franchise model, which will allow for strong revenue growth while providing a diversified revenue floor. Historically, the nephrology space has been a difficult area for investors due to the complexity of reimbursement; however, I believe the rapid increase in IBSRELA uptake indicates that Ardelyx has identified a significant gap in the irritable bowel syndrome with constipation market.

Latest Financials

Total product revenues were in excess of $378 million for the fiscal year 2025 and increased 18% versus 2024. However, the nature of the growth in the top line is significant, as the revenue generated in 2025 was primarily the result of the performance of the company's product IBSRELA, which generated in excess of $274 million for the entire year of 2025. This represents an increase of greater than 70% when compared to the same period in 2024. When a commercialized drug product reaches this level of penetration in the market within a few years of launching, the product has achieved success based upon a combination of increasing prescription rates, improved patient compliance, and repeat use.

In addition to the success of IBSRELA, the company's other product, XPHOZAH, which is used to treat hyperphosphatemia, also generated in excess of $100 million in revenue for the fiscal year 2025. Although the growth trend of XPHOZAH may not be as aggressive as that of IBSRELA, the achievement of over $100 million in revenue for a drug product that is treating a condition that is currently being treated with generic binders is a confirmation of the medical need for a novel mechanism of action. The breakdown of the performance of both products in the fourth quarter of 2025 provides additional insight into the dynamic of the company's products, with IBSRELA generating approximately $87 million and XPHOZAH generating approximately $28 million. The fact that the performance of both products in the fourth quarter of 2025 is consistent with their previous quarters indicates that the company has established stable commercialization dynamics and is experiencing predictable increases in revenue.

One of the key aspects of the company's recent financial report is its balance sheet. As most recently reported, Ardelyx had approximately $265 million in cash, cash equivalents, and investments. In guidance for 2026, Ardelyx indicated that it expects to generate between $110 and $120 million in revenue from XPHOZAH and between $410 and $430 million in revenue from IBSRELA. Therefore, the company is projecting that its two products will generate in excess of $520 million in combined product revenue in 2026 and has ample means to fund future development and expansion.

Pipeline Expansion and Developments

While the main driver for short-term value creation at Ardelyx will be the commercialization of IBSRELA, the company has simultaneously been progressing a product pipeline that utilizes the company's internal competency to inhibit NHE3 (sodium-hydrogen exchanger type 3) activity.

The major development here is that the company initiated a Phase 3 clinical trial to evaluate the safety and efficacy of IBSRELA in patients suffering from chronic idiopathic constipation. This is a strategic expansion of the highest magnitude. The mechanism of action of tenapanor is not solely limited to treating irritable bowel syndrome; its capacity to inhibit sodium absorption and enhance intestinal fluid is theoretically relevant to the larger constipation treatment space. By initiating this Phase 3 clinical trial, which is expected to be completed by the end of 2027, the company is also expanding the total addressable market for its lead product.

In addition to utilizing tenapanor to treat patients who suffer from CIC, the company is developing RDX10531, which aims to be the next generation of an NHE3 inhibitor. By developing RDX10531, the company is essentially creating its own competition by ensuring that it owns the intellectual property rights related to NHE3 inhibitors should there be a shift in standards of care relative to the use of such inhibitors in the future. While this asset is still in the early stages of its development, it provides the company with optionality and potential partnerships down the road.

Strategic Impact of Patent Extension

I believe that an underappreciated feature of the company’s latest update is the Notice of Allowance for the new patent covering the commercial formulations of both IBSRELA and XPHOZAH. The new patent expires on December 6, 2041. In standard biopharma valuation models, analysts are usually applying rapid decay to revenue after the expiration of the initial composition of matter patents.

The previous patents for IBSRELA and XPHOZAH were scheduled to expire in August 2033 and April 2034, respectively. As a result, the market has been reducing the value of the terminal value due to the impending loss of exclusivity. With the recent extension of protection through 2041, it essentially extends the peak or near-peak cash flow of the company by seven to eight years. Given that Ardelyx estimates IBSRELA to be at $1 billion in annual revenue by 2029, extending that revenue stream by ten years past the original cliff creates huge incremental value that I feel is not currently being represented in the stock price. This extension converts IBSRELA into a generational asset for the company and increases the attractiveness of the company as an acquisition target because any potential acquirer would now have a monopoly on the formulation for fifteen years.

Counterarguments and Risk Assessment

One major concern for Ardelyx may be the slow growth trajectory for XPHOZAH. Although Ardelyx has generated $104 million in revenue for the full year 2025, the growth rate of XPHOZAH is significantly lower than that of IBSRELA. Due to the use of a bundled payment system in dialysis and the inherent complexity of accessing patients (both in terms of patient flow through the clinic and regulatory compliance), the renal market has historically been very challenging to penetrate. With a revenue projection of $110 to $120 million by 2026 for XPHOZAH, Ardelyx would be implying a CAGR for XPHOZAH of approximately 6% to 15%. In stark comparison, the CAGR for IBSRELA is anticipated to be approximately 50%. Should the renal franchise experience difficulties either penetrating the market or experiencing increased reimbursement challenges, the company's valuation multiple for its equity could potentially suffer, leading to investors viewing Ardelyx as a GI company with a management team distracted by the renal franchise.

In addition to facing competitive challenges in the GI space with respect to the growth of IBSRELA, Ardelyx will face a significant challenge in achieving its $1 billion revenue goal for IBSRELA by 2029. In order to achieve this goal, IBSRELA will need to continue to displace cheap generic drugs and competing branded agents, and therefore, there exists an execution risk with respect to Ardelyx's ability to achieve this goal. Specifically, should the sales force for IBSRELA reach a saturation point with the early adopter base and fail to penetrate the mass market of general prescribers, the growth curve for IBSRELA may flatten out faster than implied by the 2029 target date.

Ardelyx's dependence upon a single mechanism of action—NHE3 inhibition—is a biological risk that represents a concentration of biological risk across all three products that are currently being developed. At this time, the safety profile for this mechanism of action is well understood; however, any emergence of long-term side effects associated with NHE3 inhibition could have the potential to jeopardize both the renal and GI space outlooks for the company.

Market Opportunity and Competitive Landscape

In the United States, IBS-C indicated net product sales of approximately $4.0 billion occurred in 2024. Growth within the market has also continued. Prescription volumes grew by 21% between 2020 and 2024. Patient satisfaction, however, is where the unique mechanism of action of IBSRELA represents a competitive barrier to entry. Only approximately 23% of the patients using prescription treatment for IBS-C do not continue to experience residual symptoms. Therefore, this is where the unique mechanism of action of IBSRELA represents a competitive barrier to entry compared to other products such as Linzess and Trulance, which act by stimulating fluid secretion via the guanylate cyclase-C receptor (a secretagogue). In contrast, IBSRELA reduces sodium absorption via local inhibition of the NHE3 transporter. As a result, this is a fundamentally different product design. In a market where patients continually try new products until finding relief, IBSRELA is able to attract those who have previously tried Linzess or Trulance and found them to be ineffective.

In terms of the renal indication, Ardelyx estimates the market size for hyperphosphatemia is over 550,000 patients on dialysis in the U.S. alone. The standard of care has traditionally been phosphate-binding agents. These agents require patients to take a high dose of pills with each meal to bind phosphorus prior to absorption. The company estimated that roughly 70% of the patients on phosphate binders are unable to keep their phosphorus levels at target. XPHOZAH does not compete as simply another phosphate binder in this space. Instead, it competes as a blocking agent that prevents absorption of phosphorus. While the generic phosphate binders may be inexpensive, there exists a ceiling effect on their efficacy, allowing XPHOZAH to be marketed as a complementary or alternative therapy for those patients who have experienced inadequate response to existing therapies.

The competitive environment in this space is somewhat less about competing with other branded drugs and more about the clinical inertia associated with traditional practices and the price sensitivity of dialysis providers. Nevertheless, XPHOZAH was able to obtain greater than $100 million in revenue during its first full year of commercialization, suggesting that nephrologists would be interested in a therapeutic agent that functions in a manner that is significantly different than the calcium or sevelamer-based binders that have been used for many years.

Future Outlook and Conclusion

I believe the market has yet to fully factor in the implications of the patent extension and the durability of the IBSRELA growth curve. Many investors look for the next big thing in biotechnology and miss the compounding value of a successful commercial launch that is currently underway before them. Ardelyx is no longer a speculative story concerning what may happen at the FDA; it is now a story about executing on the sales process and penetrating the marketplace. As such, I believe that Ardelyx presents a compelling risk-reward profile for investors that are willing to see the company through the expansionary phase of its commercial development.

data.title: Tariffs Or Greenland
data.description:
At first, my take on the Greenland issue—that is, to be clear, President Trump's demands for U.S. control of Greenland either by payment or force—was that it was another one of those topics the administration brings up and then lets die. In the "flood the zone" strategy the administration often employs, it makes sense that something as crazy as buying or even invading an ally's territory (who repeatedly has said no) would be one of the volleys cast out during times of domestic unrest to distract the news and commentators, but not actually acted on.

As events unfold, I am increasingly realizing that President Trump is serious about Greenland. There are some good reasons why Greenland is a target. While most people are focused on the critical minerals living under Greenland's glacial surface, which is a good point, I believe the real reason is military strategy. In a world where the Arctic becomes easier to traverse by ship as the ice caps continue to melt (it seems unlikely to me that it would stop anytime soon), Greenland represents one of the most strategic places to control movement in the Arctic, bar Alaska, which we already own. This view of the globe helps put Greenland's physical location into context:

The reasons why the U.S. wants Greenland might be irrelevant for now, as it appears that there is nothing the Europeans can do (or at least from what they've tried so far) to stop this train. Despite less than 20% of Americans being in support of annexing Greenland, President Trump has proven unrelenting so far.

Escalating Against Europe

In retaliation for President Trump's rather bellicose rhetoric about Greenland, in which he refused to rule out military intervention, several European countries deployed troops to the island. This group included Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the Netherlands, Finland, and the U.K.

To punish this show of solidarity with Denmark and the deployment of troops ostensibly meant to deter U.S. aggression, President Trump has put tariffs on the countries that sent soldiers to Greenland, including all the above-named. It starts at 10% and then jumps to 25% on June 1st. They go away if Greenland comes under U.S. control. In his messaging on the issue, President Trump said that the Europeans were "playing this very dangerous game," and that the level of risk is "not tenable or sustainable."

The escalated level that could come in June would be disastrous for the Europeans, who said last July that similar levels of tariffs initially put on them on Liberation Day were effectively an embargo.

So that means a jump to 25% in June would bring us back to those previous levels that are effectively embargoed; I suspect this would go poorly for markets at large, since the Eurozone (which is largely made up of the countries on this list, namely France and Germany, the largest economies in Europe) is deeply connected with the U.S. economy. Europe would likely bear the brunt of a sell-off, since they rely on the U.S. more than the U.S. relies on it in most industries, bar pharmaceuticals, which have their own tariff carve-outs.

The EU comprises a huge share of U.S. imports, over 20%. This is a massive amount of trade to disrupt, especially after the last agreement did not favor the EU; I called it a "one-sided affair."

It appears that the Europeans understand they can't just take the pain, as they are working on coming up with a solution, as Cyprus (the current president country in the EU) called an emergency meeting of all EU ambassadors. Last time this happened, their solution was to threaten the U.S. with a "trade bazooka." This was, notably, never fired, as a deal was reached shortly after. However, trade retaliation would still harm the U.S., and I expect markets to sell off on the news if Europe goes that route.

Markets Won't React Well To This Infighting

It's clear from watching the Eurozone ETF's movements how much trade impacts the bloc; see April's dip, where Europe was hit far worse than the U.S. We see a similarly deep pullback in July when the trade deal was done.

Despite this, and likely because of the removal of the worst tariffs, the Eurozone dramatically outperformed the S&P 500 last year. A further escalation around Greenland could erase much of those gains, especially if relations are permanently damaged and ties are cut. The Danes have already evoked the "end of NATO" card.

Where I expect most of the pain to show up isn't in equity markets, although they will likely open down after all this rhetoric. It's in the bond and currency markets, especially regarding U.S. treasuries. While most treasuries are held by Americans, foreign holders do have a lot of power. An EU trade bazooka being fired at us would likely lead to a mandated sell-off in sovereign holdings of UST. Europeans own $2T in treasuries and could use them as a weapon against us, and that is definitely going to be worse for markets than trade flows.

Below is the list of the largest foreign holders, with EU countries highlighted + Britain (and Cayman) and Norway, since they are targets of the new tariffs as well.

I would not be surprised if UST-based retaliation from Europe pushes long-term rates even higher, clearly above 5%. This could push almost the entire yield curve up and force the dollar lower. That would bring the Euro higher, as has already been the case since Liberation Day.

Weakness in the dollar tends to benefit the Euro, as it is considered one of the flights to safety in currencies currently. This was one of the dichotomies that has existed since 2022, when the U.S. first weaponized the dollar against Russia for its belligerence against Ukraine.

This chart comes from a recent article I wrote on gold, showing its "safe haven" status post-2022, and flows jump when geopolitics gets crazy. Interesting to see here is how non-dollar currencies have flipped, like the Swiss Franc, the Japanese Yen, and not pictured but also operating this way, the Euro.

The real winner of all of this will likely be gold, even as it continues its crazy run-up. There is a lot of geopolitical chaos in the world, amplified by moments like this where the U.S. is antagonizing its allies for territory (whether for good reasons or not, that's what is happening), and gold responds more forcefully than anything. I believe that it will continue to rise, especially if the EU doesn't give in. It looks like a stalemate over Greenland will go on for a while, and in that case, everyone involved will lose—gold will win.

Conclusion

Ultimately, I've presented a fairly grim picture of the Greenland issue, but I believe it's justified given the facts we have. No one stands to materially gain from this conflict, as the U.S. gaining Greenland won't create any more military or economic opportunity than already exists, except miners not paying Danish taxes, I suppose. Tariffs placed on Europe threaten retaliation in the treasury market, a further erosion of the dollar in currency markets (good for the Swiss Franc and Euro), and pain coming to the equity markets as traders react to the sudden news of embargo-level tariffs coming on June 1st.

The biggest winner of all of this is the asset that isn't issued or printed by either the U.S. or the EU: gold. I expect traders to continue to flee this chaos and allocate more to precious metals. Nowhere else is as safe, especially outside of equities, given the unpredictable nature of how this conflict will unfold.

It appears that neither side is willing to relent. The betting market gives the U.S. a pretty good chance at taking control (by buying or by force, not specified) at least part of the island before President Trump's term ends.

I won't be participating in that market, but those are better odds than I would give the U.S. here. I will be continuing to buy gold, however, as it will be the clear winner in this exchange, regardless of who controls Greenland in the end.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: FMI Large Cap Equity Q4 2025 Review
data.description:
Investment Philosophy:

Purchase durable business franchises that are selling at a discount to their intrinsic value.

Investment Process:

We utilize a business owner’s approach to investing, thoroughly investigating the economics of the business and the quality of the management team. Some of the characteristics of good businesses include strong recurring revenue and attractive returns on invested capital (ROIC). We have a strong orientation to low absolute and relative valuation, which are key to the execution of our investment strategy. A new idea will come from a variety of sources including company visits, screens, conferences, trade periodicals and general reading. All members of the research team are responsible for fundamental research.

Once an investment opportunity is identified it is put through an extensive due diligence process, which typically includes management interviews and site visits. When an acceptable level of conviction is achieved, the appropriate weighting (considering liquidity, valuation, etc.) is discussed and determined. A new company purchased in the portfolio will usually have an initial position size of 2-4%. The portfolio generally consists of 20-30 companies and is diversified across industries.

We are long-term investors, a typical holding period for our companies is three to five years, and portfolio turnover averages 20-40% per annum.

Recent Purchase

Arch Capital Group Ltd. Ticker: ACGL Added: November 2025

Arch Capital Group (ACGL) is a global provider of specialty insurance, reinsurance, and mortgage insurance. Their core philosophy is to cycle manage their underwriting and allocate capital to where there is more attractive (firmer) pricing. We believe this is one of the only proven ways to earn excess returns in a mostly commoditized business. Given decelerating insurance pricing and declining interest rates (i.e. peak investment income yield), Arch is entering a period of slower growth. This is a stark contrast to the last 6 years which saw P&C gross written premiums grow significantly. The change in insurance pricing has knocked Arch’s stock valuation down to an attractive level. Arch’s cycle management strategy, refined over the last 25 years, is purposely situated to navigate any pricing environment. Instead of reinvesting profits back into premium growth, we expect to see more balance across share buybacks, special dividends, and possibly M&A if the situation presents itself. We initiated a 2% position in November 2025.

Recent Sale

CarMax Inc. Ticker: KMX Sold: November 2025

Our investment thesis was that CarMax (KMX) would continue to leverage its strong business model to gain share in the fragmented used car industry, as it had done for three decades. Fundamentals over the last several years have disappointed, driven by both industry-wide affordability challenges and company-specific execution challenges. We spent considerable time re-underwriting CarMax by reviewing the physical/online/omni-channel sales process, visiting stores, talking to employees, reviewing expert transcripts and online message boards, and discussing the issues with both the CEO and Chairman. It became clear that there were structural issues in transitioning the existing business to a frictionless omni-channel offering. These issues were not yet even recognized by the management team, much less on their way to being fixed. In our discussions it became clear that the cyclical headwinds were compounded by business model challenges and management. This was confirmed in the most recent quarterly results which showed significant volume declines and cost deleverage. Given the lack of a strategic plan, they are cutting price and increasing marketing to reaccelerate sales growth. We believe this will be grossly insufficient to return the company to sustainable growth. We sold our 1.9% position in November 2025.

data.title: Why Markets And The Economy In 2026 May End Up Looking A Lot Like 2025
data.description:
With 2026 underway, there is a lot for investors to navigate. TD Cowen has come out with its Best Ideas 2026 report, and among the key takeaways - this year is likely going to look a lot like last year. Lisa Thomas, Managing Director, Deputy Head of Global Research at TD Cowen, discusses the main macro themes to watch.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: With 2026 underway, there's a lot for investors to navigate through. But TD Cowen has come out with its "Best Ideas of 2026," and one of the key takeaways is that this year is going to look a lot like last year.

So joining us now to break down the big themes and also some stocks to watch, Lisa Thomas of TD Cowen joins us now. Lisa, nice to see you.

Lisa Thomas: Great to see you. Happy New Year.

Kim Parlee: Happy New Year to you too. Why do you think 2026 looks more like a continuation of 2025, and that something different is not going to happen?

Lisa Thomas: Sure. Certainly already in the first couple of weeks of the year, we and markets are digesting a lot in terms of news flow. But when you do break it down and spend time with our Washington policy analysts, I think that there really is a clear continuation of the America First agenda that President Trump and his administration had started to implement back in January of last year.

I think we see that on the geopolitical front, as well as on the domestic side. And so, again, it feels like a lot to react to, but it's very consistent in terms of the direction.

Kim Parlee: I think a lot of people were quite surprised by the resilience of many economies last year. And I think you see that continuing. You see from the numbers that you put in your report, the global economy is going to continue to grow. The US economy will continue to outperform. So where is the resilience coming from?

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Part of the resilience comes from productivity that is really a realization of productivity from workers who were hired coming out of the end of COVID. So, as you recall, there was a tremendous amount of hiring during that period. And it takes a while for that to really realize at a whole economic level. I think that we're seeing that now, and that's part of the gain.

But another thing that I'd call out is fiscal policy. The president passed last year in the US the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. And with that, we are expecting a tremendous uptick in federal tax refunds for US consumers and households this year.

Those refunds at a household level could go up by 40-45%. It's about $150 billion in the US projected. So those kinds of things, that addition to worker productivity, and then household spending being fueled as well by tax refunds. Both of those things are going to help keep the economy moving.

Kim Parlee: I certainly would like a 40-45% increase in my tax refund, but I don't think that's happening. But anyway, let's talk about Canada. And when you look at the Canadian outlook, it's powered by something different and you're focused a lot more on the fiscal policy side of things.

Lisa Thomas: That's right. I mean, that's really a function of budget increases that for this year are 7% versus last, '26 over '25. That's going to make a big difference. That's $30 billion for this year, which alone could add 80 basis points-- nearly a full percentage of GDP just from that increase in the budget.

And that's not something that is going to be standing alone for a year. There's a five-year projection that is going to add just under $90 billion to the economy in Canada.

Kim Parlee: What are the risks? And if you wouldn't mind, I wouldn't mind talking about Canada first and then we can talk about the States. Obviously, risk to the States will also be risk to Canada. But it's an interesting time. So, Canada.

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Certainly trade remains a wild card. I think that we believe that net net, that the increases to spending through the fiscal policy will outweigh any drag caused by trade. We would flag that. You've still got about 90% of all trade is USMCA-compliant under exemptions.

And when we talk to our Canadian strategists, they're very eager to point to the fact that the continuation of those exemptions, the fact that they are in there, is a signal that should be read as saying that the US administration does want to see those continue to be in place.

So, it's entirely possible that the USMCA that we land with is going to look a lot like the last one, and that would be a benefit.

Kim Parlee: That really, really would be a benefit. What about the US? I know that you highlight in the report execution risk. And maybe just tell me a bit about what specifically you mean by that.

Lisa Thomas: Sure thing. There's certainly a lot of expectation that rests on the deregulation part of the president's agenda in the US. And where we see execution risk actually comes out in some of the news flow, in fact, that we've seen this week with the discussion around Chairman Powell and the investigation-- the DOJ investigation into the Fed and Chairman Powell that's currently in discussion.

The expectation had been that Chair Powell would step down from the Fed when his chairman term expires this May. But I think if he believes that there's an ongoing threat to Fed independence, it's certainly possible that he might remain as a Fed governor.

And that could actually make the picture more complicated when you're thinking about the Fed rate cuts that the market is expecting for this year.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, it really does-- the opposite impact, perhaps, of what the president was expecting. Can I ask you a bit about longer-term trends, too? I mean, we always want to know, it's convenient to talk about 2026 and to hear what's coming up, but I know you've got your eye on some longer-term trends as well. And you're highlighting demographics. So maybe tell us a bit about that.

Lisa Thomas: Sure. We're highlighting demographics again this year in our TD Cowen 2026 themes book, which is really the piece in which we take a big step back, open the aperture and look at those longer-term trends. When we were talking about some of the points earlier, those are really things that are subject to change, they're policy-driven, and sometimes people can be whipsawed.

But when you're thinking about things like a population, an aging population, that's something that we can see into the future and know is happening. So, for example, the aging population is something we have to pay a lot of attention to.

This past year, 2025, was the peak year for 18-year-olds in the US. From 2025 on, that number is going to decline. So you're going to have almost 2.5 million fewer 18-year-olds in less than a decade. In less than a decade, the death rate in the US will actually exceed the birth rate.

So we're really starting to think about investments and investment strategies that take account of that aging population, and the fact that you're going to have fewer younger people, younger workers, and then also can address the needs of an aging population, 65-plus. Because that population, 65-plus, is going to be 23% of the US population by 2050.

Kim Parlee: You were talking about demographics, and my probably less precise summary of it is just, everyone's aging. Everyone's getting older and we're not replenishing.

Lisa Thomas: With every day.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. So, the first idea you have from "Best Ideas 2026 Report" that you put out-- and I should mention you're putting out quite a few, we're going to talk to just a few of them-- is Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS).

Lisa Thomas: That's right. Ionis Pharmaceuticals is the Best Idea from our biotechnology analyst, Yaron Werber. And I think that biopharma innovation is the natural place to go when you're thinking about an aging population.

So we like Ionis because it has a number of data catalysts coming up this year. Based on our checks with physicians and experts in the field, we think that the likelihood of success in a number of those is better than understood by investors.

And they do, in particular, have some indications that relate to things like cardiomyopathy, which is a problem that is faced by a large number of people in the 60-75 population-- so a large market, again, related to aging.

And they also are working on some indications in partnership with another pharma company, that would address proteins that are relevant to early onset Alzheimer's.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And I'll just mention to people, as you say in the report, I think it's the ticker on this one, IONS. And then we also have current price around $81, price target around $99. And a reminder to everybody, do your own research. It has to make sense for whatever you're doing.

OK, the second one in terms of Best Ideas for 2026, Royalty Pharmaceuticals (RPRX).

Lisa Thomas: Yes. Royalty Pharmaceuticals is an interesting one. That's another Best Idea that's been advanced by our pharma analyst, Michael Nedelcovych. And Mike likes the stock because Royalty Pharma gives you exposure to biopharma innovation, but without that risk of binary outcomes in a clinical trial, and without the operational risk around running a pharma business.

So Royalty Pharma purchases, Royalty revenue stream rights, they can build a portfolio across markets and indications, including indications relevant to aging population. And they also are better able to manage patent risk than an actual pharma operating company.

So your return profile is going to be still projected to be 10% compounded over the next five years, according to our analyst projections. But that's with a lot of risk managed to the downside given the different structure versus owning directly in pharma.

Kim Parlee: Interesting. I think the streams of royalties is something that's familiar to a lot of Canadians through mining.

Lisa Thomas: That's right.

Kim Parlee: This time, though, through pharma--

Lisa Thomas: That's right.

Kim Parlee: --which is interesting. And again, just the ticker's on the screen for people to see it. Current trading in around $38.39 and price target of $45. Again, do your own research and make sure it makes sense for you.

OK, Apollo Global Management (APO) is your third one we're going to talk about today.

Lisa Thomas: Thank you. Yeah, I think Apollo Global Management is a really important stock to think about when you're thinking about an aging population and growth of that retiree population, bearing in mind that in the US you've got 12,000 people every day who are turning age 65.

And for any survey that's run on a retiree population, consistently, at least a third of those respondents will tell you that running out of money in retirement is a chief concern. So our analyst, Bill Katz, likes Apollo because they're particularly diverse in offering a range of guaranteed income and annuity products that are particularly well-suited for that population.

And I would say they also have upside as an alternative asset manager, because they also give exposure to some of that democratization of wealth that we're seeing, where you have things like private credit and other vehicles that used to just be available to institutional investors, can now go into retiree account investing as well.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And I want to squeeze one in really fast. A name familiar to everybody, unless you've been hiding under a rock, Amazon (AMZN).

Lisa Thomas: That's absolutely right. Amazon is a stock that our internet analyst, John Blackledge, is advancing as his Best Idea this year, in large part because of the AWS business segment, which is, of course, the backbone of AI infrastructure.

Since 2022, AWS has doubled its capacity, and they expect to be able to do that again by 2027. They have found that the demand for AI infrastructure capacity continues to be insatiable. And so, that, by our forecasts over the last 12 months, is roughly a $90 billion business-- just that segment alone.

I think that that's something that we need to think about around an aging population, because it's not just that we're building the infrastructure for its own sake-- that has to support need. And when you're thinking about mobility needs for an elderly population that's increasingly going to rely on automation.

And similarly, if you have fewer younger workers, more and more automation solutions are going to be needed to power output and productivity. And that is going to be very reliant on AI.

Kim Parlee: Alright. And just to mention again to everybody, current price, say $220, high $220s, I'll say that. Could be beyond that now. And price target of $300. But again, do your own research.

Lisa, it's always great to have you with us. Appreciate you bringing this to us.

Lisa Thomas: Likewise. Thanks so much. Take care now.

Kim Parlee: Yeah, you, too. That is Lisa Thomas of TD Cowen joining us tonight. And a reminder, as I've been saying all along, please do your own research. Make sure it makes sense for you.

data.title: Akamai Technologies: Growing Edge Opportunities In The Agentic AI Era
data.description:
Akamai Technologies, Inc. (AKAM), a pioneer in the Content Delivery Network (CDN), is a stock I have been following for years. During my first coverage of the stock in July 2020, the prevailing trend was the massive COVID-19-related surge in global internet traffic that ended up accelerating demand for AKAM's cloud security and CDN services.

Suffice it to say, the stock has not performed well since my first coverage, which may have to do with AKAM's continuous transformation into a cloud edge inference and security player, followed by the consistent decline in its legacy CDN business.

In the past five years, in fact, AKAM has been trading sideways and has been down over -12%. Nonetheless, the stock has gained some renewed momentum YTD as it trades around $93 per share as of late, possibly driven by the recent expansion into the AI inference cloud market.

I maintain my buy rating. My 1-year price target of $108.2 presents a 16% upside from the current price of $93.5 per share.

Financial Reviews

AKAM still appears to be in a transition into a newer, yet specialized edge inference cloud and security business.

Q3 revenue was $1.06 billion, a 5% YoY growth, driven primarily by two business segments. CIS (Cloud Infrastructure Services) saw a 39% YoY revenue growth to $81 million, while security revenue grew 10% YoY to $568 million. Both segments' growth offset the declining legacy CDN business, which fell 4% YoY to $306 million.

Meanwhile, profitability grew stronger in Q3, driven by operational efficiencies as well as the growth of higher-margin CIS business. In addition to Non-GAAP EPS growing by 17% YoY to $1.86, non-GAAP operating margins also saw a 31% improvement.

Liquidity and balance sheet remain solid. While the debt-to-equity ratio inched higher, at 1.1x, it is still relatively at a healthy level. AKAM ended Q3 with $1.8 billion of cash and short-term investments, with a significant contribution coming from its cash flow from operations (OCF), which was over $1.1 billion in the nine months ending Q3, similar to the same level last year.

I believe that the strong OCF, as well as the balance sheet, provide AKAM with enough dry powder to heavily invest in CAPEX to drive its new business. In that sense, AKAM is on track to achieve its target of investing in CAPEX for CIS infrastructure at 20% of its revenue target for the FY as per its earlier guidance. In the end, around $800 million was also spent in the first nine months of 2025 for share repurchases.

Catalyst

I see a few catalysts that could help AKAM maintain its uptrend share performance YTD as it continues its shift from a CDN to a cloud and security player. Given the declining legacy CDN business, these catalysts are primarily the growing trends around AI that may positively affect its CIS and Cloud Security business.

First off, I expect AKAM's edge AI inference technology to see growing demand due to many real-time use cases in enterprise AI that require better latency. Essentially, I would imagine many companies deploying agentic AI models into their operations across functions, demanding better connectivity.

As per the report by Gartner, 40% of enterprise applications will leverage AI agents in 2026, a 5% increase YoY. As such, while the cloud market appears to be saturated with players like Amazon AWS and Microsoft Azure, the AI inference cloud seems to be an underrated opportunity in 2026.

Therefore, in my opinion, while cloud security, AKAM's largest business today, continues to benefit from the increasing AI-driven cyber threats and grow in the low teens, AKAM's CIS business may continue to see sustained high growth around the 30% range, driving valuation re-rating.

Furthermore, the fact that AKAM only saw a -4% decline in the legacy CDN segment in Q3, which is a lower sequential decline compared to the previous two quarters, suggests that the CDN decline may be close to bottoming out. As a result, if the double-digit growth across CIS and Cloud Security is maintained throughout 2026, AKAM could soon be seeing less negative impact from the CDN decline, driving guidance beats.

Risk

Overall, risk seems minimal. While the 2026 outlook appears interesting, for instance, my view is that there is a likelihood that the market for edge cloud AI inference is developing more slowly than expected in 2026, since, despite the real AI trend, the enterprise agentic AI use cases are, for the most part, still in early stages. In that sense, companies would probably choose to deploy their inference AI inside their centralized hyperscaler servers, such as AWS or Azure, despite the latency benefits of edge cloud AI.

In the worst-case scenario, given the continued decline of CDN, the relatively high CAPEX investment AKAM has made to scale its CIS business, and the high-growth rate of the new CIS business that has been supporting the bullish case for re-rating, AKAM may struggle to earn its market premium into 2026.

Valuation/Pricing

My target price for AKAM is driven by the following assumptions for the bull vs bear scenarios of the FY 2026 projection based on the consensus estimate:

  1. Bull scenario (60% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth accelerating to more than 40% YoY as more enterprises are adopting AKAM's edge AI approach to reduce latency, while the legacy CDN decline continues to slow down. I give AKAM a forward P/S of 4x, at the higher end of its historical trading range, suggesting that it should transition more into being valued as a cloud AI or security instead of a commodity CDN player.

  2. Bear scenario (40% probability) assumptions—AKAM sees CIS growth stagnant, with the heavy CAPEX investment that has been around 20% of revenue failing to yield the desired ROI. More enterprises choose centralized clouds instead of the edge to deploy AI models. I assign AKAM a forward P/S of 2.7x, not at the lowest end of its valuation range, but also lower than where it is today, suggesting that any hiccups in the new business means that it could more or less still be considered a commodity CDN competitor.

Consolidating all the information above into my model, I arrived at an FY 2026 weighted target price of $108.2 per share, presenting a potential upside of 16% from the current level. At this point, I would give the stock a buy rating.

Conclusion

AKAM enters 2026 with an attractive market narrative, successfully transforming itself from a commodity CDN competitor into a fast-growing cloud security and AI edge inference player. With the two newer businesses thriving in the growing market due to the enterprise AI trend, I have a strong belief that AKAM could potentially see a re-rating in 2026. Despite the risk of hyperscalers taking the share of the AI edge market by offering a centralized alternative to enterprises, which I have built into my price target analysis, I think that risk remains minimal, and at this price point, AKAM is an attractive buy.

data.title: Carillon Eagle Growth & Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The equity market ended 2025 on a positive note. The S&P 500 Index returned 2.7% in the fourth quarter, finishing the year up 17.9%, just shy of a third consecutive 20%+ annual gain. This outcome seemed unlikely early in the year amid several potential disruptions, including the emergence of new competitive artificial intelligence (AI) models from China and heightened uncertainty following the rollout of a new U.S. tariff regime. Ultimately, strong AI‑related investment, the resumption of U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts, and a more pragmatic tone from the Trump administration on trade negotiations allowed equities to push to new highs.

As in the prior two years, the communication services and technology sectors led performance, with a narrow set of mega‑cap technology, media, and telecommunications stocks contributing to approximately half of the S&P 500’s return. Still, market breadth improved meaningfully relative to 2023 and 2024. All 11 sectors posted positive returns, and the S&P 500® Equal Weight Index rose 11.4%. While this lagged the cap‑weighted index by 650 basis points (bps), the gap narrowed considerably from the 12% to 13% differentials of the prior two years. Notably, the top‑performing equity factor in 2025 was positive 3‑month earnings per share (EPS) revisions, which outpaced momentum, size, and growth. We view this as evidence that fundamentals still matter.

From a return‑driver standpoint, earnings growth was the clear engine of the market’s advance in 2025. Forward S&P 500 earnings are projected to rise 16% in 2026 over 2025. Dividends contributed roughly 1.5% to the total return, while valuation remained largely unchanged, with the index ending the year at approximately 22 times next 12‑month earnings.

Portfolio Review

Top securities

AstraZeneca (ANZ) reported third‑quarter financial results that surpassed analyst expectations and reaffirmed its outlook for operating margin improvements in 2026. The company also announced a constructive drug pricing agreement with the Trump administration, and presented impressive clinical trial results for a new drug to treat hypertension, which the company expects to have on the market by mid‑2026.

Lam Research (LRCX) shares benefitted from improving sentiment regarding the importance of its products within the semiconductor capital spending market. Following disappointments from semiconductor peers, Lam Research confirmed growth projections for next year, supported by bookings data. As a leading provider of equipment tied to memory requirements for AI, we believe Lam Research could have a long and healthy path to growth moving forward.

Goldman Sachs Group’s (GS) shares contributed to the fourth‑quarter performance due to positive financial results, coupled with increased optimism regarding capital markets activity heading into 2026. Goldman Sachs maintains one of the strongest global merger and acquisition (M&A) advisory and trading, with increased activity in M&A, initial public offerings, and debt issuance activity directly boosting its financial performance.

RTX’s (RTX) positive performance was a result of a strong third quarter earnings report that demonstrated improved revenue and earnings growth. Key end markets such as commercial aerospace and global defense are poised to continue to push excess growth for the company over the next several years. As such, we remain positive on RTX.

Analog Devices (ADI) pushed toward new all‑time highs after solid earnings gave investors' confidence that the analog cycle is now beyond its bottom. We believe the cycle is on track for an early recovery in 2026.

Bottom securities

Oracle (ORCL) gave up some of its gains after a big run to start the year over concerns about choppiness in AI spending and valuation pushbacks.

Eaton’s (ETN) shares pulled back in August following strong performance through July. Like many companies in the industrials complex, Eaton’s results did not exceed very high investor expectations. Moving into 2026, we anticipate accelerated growth for the company that is tied to strong trends in data center, aerospace, and defense markets.

Microsoft (MSFT) delivered weak quarterly performance, driven by industry‑wide concerns over a slowdown in software seat licenses, a key worry among investors.

T‑Mobile (TMUS) lagged amid uncertainty surrounding the strategic direction of one of its largest competitors. A new CEO at the competing company appears to have a more aggressive posture, which could pressure net subscription additions for T‑Mobile. However, in historical times of elevated switching behavior, T‑Mobile has tended to gain market share due to its well‑established value proposition.

Home Depot (HD) traded lower after reporting weaker‑than‑expected same‑store sales for the prior quarter. While weather‑related events boosted results in the prior quarter, the additional demand management anticipated failed to materialize. On a positive note, recent acquisitions continue to perform well and appear likely to be a tailwind as the business environment improves.

Outlook

With the S&P 500 up 86% for the past three years, it is reasonable to question the runway for further gains. However, as we enter 2026, we see more tangible tailwinds than headwinds and believe another year of positive equity returns is achievable.

Earnings remain the core pillar. Bloomberg projects S&P 500 EPS growth of 16% in 2026, up from 15% in 2025 – with 7 of 11 sectors expected to deliver double‑digit gains. AI investment remains robust and has the potential to broaden its impact across industries. Meanwhile, although tariffs remain a drag on year‑over‑year comparisons, companies have now had nine months since Liberation Day to redesign supply chains, adjust pricing, or mitigate cost pressures. The tariff anniversary mid‑year could further ease the growth impact. The macroeconomic backdrop remains constructive. Inflation continues to slow, the labor market is cooling without collapsing, and corporate profitability remains resilient. If the Fed maintains its data‑driven stance, monetary policy could be expected to gradually become more accommodative.

According to Bloomberg, futures contracts currently imply two 25‑bp cuts in 2026, which we view as a conservative starting point. Consumer strength remains a swing factor. A K‑shaped pattern persists, with lower‑income households pressured by the cumulative impact of inflation and softer wage growth. High‑income consumers – supported by wealth effects from rising asset prices – continue to spend consistently, and we do not see evidence this trend will change. Additionally, fiscal policies in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act should provide incremental stimulus beginning in early 2026.

Frothy equity valuations are often cited as a risk to the current bull market. At 22x forward earnings, the S&P 500 trades within one multiple of its 25‑year high. However, the market sustained this multiple throughout 2025, and accelerating earnings growth may further justify it. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index trades at a more modest 17.5x, suggesting the “average” stock is far less stretched and has the potential for broader market participation. The AI supercycle continues to provide powerful support, yet it carries risks. Investor willingness to underwrite aggressive AI spending has cooled somewhat, and the debate over whether we are in an AI bubble has increased. We expect AI‑related stock volatility to persist in 2026, but as long as forward revenue and earnings estimates continue to rise, the multi‑year AI investment cycle should remain intact. Policy remains a wildcard. With the unconventional administration, sudden shifts in tone or direction are possible. That said, mid‑term election years often bring a degree of policy restraint, which could reduce headline risk. Monetary policy uncertainty is another factor, particularly if inflation re‑accelerates. A new Fed chair is expected mid‑year, and this may also shape the policy path.

Risk Considerations:

International investing presents specific risks, such as currency fluctuations, differences in financial accounting standards as well as potential political and economic instability.

Because the fund normally will hold a focused portfolio of stocks of fewer companies than many other diversified funds, the increase or decrease of the value of a single stock may have a greater impact on the fund’s net asset value and total return.

As with all equity investing, there is the risk that an unexpected change in the market or within the company itself may have an adverse effect on its stock. The biggest risk of equity investing is that returns can fluctuate and investors can lose money.

There are risks associated with dividend investing, including that dividend‑issuing companies may choose not to pay a dividend, may not have the ability to pay, or the dividend may be less than what is anticipated. Dividend issuing companies are subject to interest rate risk and high dividends can sometimes signal that a company is in distress.

Growth companies are expected to increase their earnings at a certain rate. When these expectations are not met, investors may punish the stocks excessively, even if earnings showed an absolute increase. Growth company stocks also typically lack the dividend yield that can cushion stock prices in market downturns. The companies engaged in the technology industry are subject to fierce competition and their products and services may be subject to rapid obsolescence. The values of these companies tend to fluctuate sharply.

Investments in mid‑cap and small‑cap companies generally involve greater risks than investing in larger capitalization companies. Mid‑cap companies often have narrower commercial markets, more limited managerial and financial resources, and more volatile trading than larger, more established companies.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITS) may be affected by economic conditions including credit risk, interest rate risk and other factors that affect property values, rents or occupancies of real estate.

All entities named are affiliates.

Definitions

Artificial intelligence (AI) ‑ technology that enables computers and machines to simulate human learning, comprehension, problem solving, decision making, creativity and autonomy.

Basis points (bps) – Measurements used in discussions of interest rates and other percentages in finance. One basis point is equal to 1/100th of 1%, or 0.01%.

Headwind – An event or market force that hinders the prospects for performance in an individual investment or group of investments. K‑shaped economy – a situation where, following an economic downturn, different parts of the economy recover at different rates, creating a divergence like the letter “K.”

Macroeconomic ‑ the branch of economics that focuses on seeking to understand the interactions between the markets, businesses, governments, and consumers that make up an entire economy.

Multiple, price multiple, earnings multiple – A measure of a company’s value based on the ratio of its current share price to its earnings per share. This ratio is known as the price‑to‑earnings ratio, or P/E.

Next‑12‑months prices/earnings ratio (NTM P/E) – A version of the ratio of price‑to‑earnings that uses forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation.

One Big Beautiful Bill Act – An act passed and signed into law in July 2025 that raised the U.S. debt ceiling by $5 trillion, made permanent tax cuts created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, and changed a wide range of other aspects of federal tax policy, making changes to health insurance legislation, phasing out or reducing credits for clean energy production or use, and removing tax benefits for illegal immigrants.

Tailwind – An event or market force that exerts a positive influence on an investment's performance. The opposite of a tailwind is a headwind, which contributes to an investment’s underperformance.

Total return includes interest, capital gains, dividends, and distributions realized over the specified period. distributions, or dividends and capital appreciation, representing the change in the market price of an asset.
